questions.scenario:
After release `2026-05-02.4`, CloudWatch Logs cost rises faster than request traffic. The app now writes this line on every normal checkout:

```text
DEBUG route=POST /v1/orders request_id=req_62 payloadBytes=18421 message=request payload accepted
```

Which cost shape should the team discuss first?

options.text: Growth cost, because log storage accumulates over time even when each request emits the expected volume.
Idle cost, because retained log groups continue to incur storage cost between requests.
Hidden cost, because application behavior created extra log ingestion and storage around normal traffic.
Direct cost, because CloudWatch ingestion is an explicit billed service used by the workload.

questions.explanation:
Hidden cost often comes from behavior around the main resource. A chatty debug log line can multiply CloudWatch ingestion and storage without adding much diagnostic value. The fix should preserve useful request evidence while reducing noise.

Learn more in [CloudWatch Logs concepts](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/logs/WhatIsCloudWatchLogs.html).

questions.scenario: A bad write corrupts order totals at 10:12 UTC. The business says checkout must be safe again within 30 minutes and no more than about 5 minutes of accepted order writes can be lost.

options.text: Use a 5-minute RTO and a 30-minute RPO because the smaller number should control the restoration sequence.
Use a 5-minute RTO and 30-minute RPO because the corruption time determines the service recovery deadline.
Set both objectives to 30 minutes because recovery cannot be more precise than the incident detection time.
The 30-minute target is RTO, and the 5-minute data-loss target is RPO.

questions.explanation:
RTO is about how long recovery can take. RPO is about how fresh recovered data must be, or how much recent data loss is acceptable. A backup setting only matters if it can actually meet those targets.

Learn more in [AWS disaster recovery options](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/wellarchitected/latest/reliability-pillar/plan-for-disaster-recovery-dr.html).

questions.scenario:
A log review finds this normal request pair:

```text
DEBUG route=POST /v1/orders request_id=req_20 body_size=18421 message=request payload accepted
INFO route=POST /v1/orders request_id=req_20 status=201 duration_ms=84 message=order created
```

Which change best balances cost and resilience?

options.text: Remove or sample the noisy debug payload while keeping useful INFO fields and error details.
Retain both lines but shorten log-group retention so current incident evidence stays unchanged.
Sample successful INFO events while keeping every DEBUG payload because failures need request bodies for diagnosis.
Remove request IDs from successful events but keep full request payloads so storage falls without losing context.

questions.explanation:
The INFO line has operational value: request ID, route, status, duration, and result. The debug payload is likely expensive and noisy during normal traffic. A good change keeps diagnosis evidence while reducing avoidable ingestion.

Learn more in [CloudWatch Logs concepts](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/logs/WhatIsCloudWatchLogs.html).

questions.scenario: During a quarterly review, the orders service shows three distinct cost movements: receipt and log storage grows with order history, snapshots and restore drills are retained to meet recovery commitments, and a retry defect multiplies worker invocations plus debug-log volume. The team wants to assign owners before choosing reductions. Which classification best supports that review?

options.text: Classify retained recovery controls as recovery cost, historical storage growth as growth cost, and retry amplification as hidden cost.
Classify all three movements as direct capacity cost because each one appears on the AWS bill.
Classify snapshots and restore drills as hidden waste because they do not serve normal checkout traffic.
Classify retry amplification as growth cost because its spend rises over time, and delete the associated logs first.

questions.explanation:
The three movements have different causes and therefore different owners and remedies. Historical storage grows with the workload, recovery controls intentionally buy recoverability, and retries multiply work without adding customer value. Treating every billed item as equivalent would encourage cuts that weaken recovery while leaving the defect untouched.

Learn more in [Analyzing costs with Cost Explorer](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cost-management/latest/userguide/ce-what-is.html).

questions.scenario: A DevPolaris manager proposes deleting 30-day API logs and cancelling RDS restore drills because neither control serves normal checkout traffic. Support incidents often arrive 7 to 14 days after the original order, and the last restore drill found a missing database security-group rule before an actual outage. How should the team classify and review these costs?

options.text: Keep both controls unchanged because one past incident proved that their current cost is justified indefinitely.
Those are recovery and evidence costs with named jobs, so tune them intentionally rather than deleting them blindly.
Reduce log retention first because restore testing provides stronger evidence than historical application logs.
Keep retention unchanged but stop restore drills once one recovery succeeds within the target.

questions.explanation:
Recovery cost can look wasteful on quiet days because it protects future failures. The key is to name the job: support investigation, incident review, and tested recovery. Once the reason is explicit, the team can tune retention and drill cadence safely.

Learn more in [AWS disaster recovery options](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/wellarchitected/latest/reliability-pillar/plan-for-disaster-recovery-dr.html).

questions.scenario: Checkout returns 201, but receipt emails are delayed because the `devpolaris-receipt-email` Lambda is failing. The API is usable, but customers do not receive receipts.

options.text: Treat checkout as available and classify receipt delivery against its own service objective and recovery owner.
Declare full checkout downtime because a user-visible part of the transaction is delayed.
Treat the email failure as accepted data loss if the queue retains messages for later processing.
This is degraded service, not necessarily full checkout downtime, and it still deserves an owned recovery path.

questions.explanation:
Resilience depends on user impact. Orders may complete while a side job is degraded, so the urgency and response differ from checkout 500s. The team should still know how to recover the side job and communicate impact.

Learn more in [AWS Lambda concepts](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/welcome.html).

questions.scenario:
DevPolaris is reviewing a cost increase before changing production capacity. Cost Explorer and the release record show the following movements.

Time window is last 14 days. Changed signals: ECS and Fargate usage is steady high baseline after desired count increase. RDS usage is steady after database resize. CloudWatch usage is rising after debug logs were enabled. S3 usage is gradual growth in object versions. First engineering question is which changes were deliberate resilience buffers, and which are leftovers?

What should the team do before cutting resources?

options.text: Map each cost movement to usage, health, and the protection it was meant to buy.
Reduce the largest cost line first, then use incident metrics to verify whether the cut was safe.
Separate deliberate buffers from leftovers, then remove the leftovers together so the next bill shows a clear reduction.
Freeze all cost changes until the next recovery exercise provides a full production benchmark.

questions.explanation:
Cost Explorer shows where spend moved, but it does not say which movement is safe to reduce. The team needs runtime, traffic, error, retention, and recovery context. That separates useful buffers from leftovers.

Learn more in [Analyzing costs with Cost Explorer](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cost-management/latest/userguide/ce-what-is.html).
